sources.name: Wikipedia — Ghouls 'n Ghosts
sources.notes: Genesis port mechanics. Key difference from NES GnG: can fire in reverse direction, Golden Armor magic.

notes: Ghouls 'n Ghosts (GhG) is the 1988 arcade sequel to Ghosts 'n Goblins (1985). The Genesis version is a home port of the arcade game.
KEY MECHANIC DIFFERENCE FROM NES GnG: Arthur can fire in the REVERSE direction by pressing down+fire while jumping. This allows targeting enemies behind him mid-jump.
KEY ADDITION: Golden Armor — third armor tier (Bronze → Steel → Golden). Golden Armor allows magic attacks.
Still uses COMMITTED FIXED ARC jumps — no horizontal air control. Same core limitation as NES GnG.
Still uses TWO-HIT death system: armor hit → underwear → next hit = death.
Still requires TWO PLAYTHROUGHS for the true ending — same design lineage as GnG.
Improved arcade-to-home conversion: Genesis version is considered superior to contemporaneous NES/Famicom GhG ports.

walk_speed.notes: Arthur is not fast. Movement feel inherited from GnG series. VERIFY.

jump._note: Same committed arc system as NES GnG. Core limitation unchanged.

reverse_fire.description: KEY ADDITION OVER NES GnG: While jumping, press down+fire to fire in the REVERSE direction — the direction Arthur came from.
reverse_fire.effect: Arthur fires backward while maintaining his jump arc forward.
notes: This addresses the core vulnerability of the committed jump: enemies approaching from behind.
In NES GnG, an enemy behind Arthur during a jump was essentially untouchable.
The reverse fire adds a safety valve without changing the core committed-arc design.
Directional firing adds a new skill: identifying threats BEHIND you and responding mid-arc.

jump.notes: No horizontal control even in GhG — the committed arc persists. Only the firing direction was added.

armor_system._note: Three-tier armor system — same concept as NES GnG but with Golden Armor added.

golden_armor.description: NEW IN GhG (not in NES GnG). Third armor. Enables magical attacks when carrying appropriate weapon.

magic_attack.description: When wearing Golden Armor, holding fire charges a magic blast that clears the screen or hits multiple enemies.
notes: Magic attack type depends on current weapon.
The Golden Armor's magic is the power fantasy payoff for mastery — rare to have all three armor upgrades.

underwear_state.description: Losing all armor leaves Arthur in underwear. Same as NES GnG.

weapons._note: Similar weapon set to NES GnG but with additions.
weapons.torch: Arcing path — lights enemies on fire
notes: Weapon is changed by collecting chests (same as NES GnG).
Different weapons interact with the Golden Armor's magic in different ways.
Players often prefer certain weapons and will intentionally avoid chest pickups to keep preferred weapons.

knockback.description: Taking damage sends Arthur flying backward.
notes: Same as NES GnG — knockback into a pit = death regardless of armor.
The knockback during the committed jump arc can create cascading falls.
Enemies are often positioned to maximize knockback into hazards.

two_playthrough_design.description: First playthrough ends with a fake final boss/ending. Arthur must complete the game again (second loop) to reach the true final boss.
notes: Identical design principle to NES GnG — inherited from the arcade era.
The second loop may have different enemy patterns or requirements (VERIFY for GhG specifically).
The two-playthrough requirement is the series' most notorious design choice.

changes: Reverse fire (backward shot while jumping) — most significant gameplay change
Golden Armor added (third tier, magic attacks)
16-bit hardware — larger sprites, more detailed backgrounds, parallax scrolling
Different level design (not a port of NES GnG — original arcade sequel)
unchanged: Committed fixed arc jump — no horizontal air control

design_notes_for_giants_drink: The reverse-fire addition to GhG is a careful surgical change: it addresses the specific vulnerability of the committed jump (enemies behind you) without altering the core constraint (no horizontal control). This is 'precision addition' — adding exactly enough to fix the specific problem without changing the design philosophy.

Golden Armor's magic attack creates a reward for mastery: players who navigate three armor upgrades and maintain them get access to a screen-clearing power most players never see. The magic is the reward for surviving long enough to collect three chests while taking no damage — a hidden high-difficulty reward.

The two-playthrough true ending becomes a tradition in GhG, making it feel intentional rather than cruel by the second game. Arcade players were already used to 'loop' structures. The home console version inherits this — a design choice that made more sense in its original context.

GhG's jump design gives Arthur a choice on every jump: fire forward (default) or fire backward (down+fire). This creates a read-and-react decision within the fixed arc: is the bigger threat in front of me or behind me? This is micro-strategy within the constrained jump.

The Ghouls 'n Ghosts series is uniquely committed to its core constraint — the committed arc jump. Three games (GnG, GhG, SGnG) maintained it across two hardware generations. The Super version added a second jump but kept both arcs committed. The series is an argument that a severe constraint can be the game's identity.
